summary: The chapter continues describing the lottery system for Temple services. First: the ramp height calculation (30 amot, 32 amot, or 28 amot?). Second: the four lotteries are listed and their procedures. Third: a long biblical narrative digression on Saul and David (continuing from 22a) covering key incidents. Fourth: the narrative continues with the dispute between Saul and David over the size of their guilt.

display.whats: A brief halakhic calculation: the altar ramp (kevesh) was 32 amot long according to R. Yosi b. R. Yehuda (30 amot rise plus 1 amah for the edge + 1 for the top). The discussion of the ramp's dimensions is relevant because the race up the ramp defined the original service competition.
display.hint: The precise calculation of the ramp dimensions is typical Talmudic interest in the technical details of Temple construction. The 30/32/28 amot dispute reflects different traditions about the ramp's exact dimensions.

learning.summary: The altar ramp (kevesh) height: R. Yosi b. R. Yehuda calculates 32 amot (30 amot rise + 1 for the rim + 1 for the body). This is relevant background to the ramp race described in the previous mishna.
learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara insists on precise measurements even for a ramp that no longer exists. This is characteristic of Talmudic approach: even destroyed Temple elements are studied in full technical detail.
learning.memoryAnchor: Ramp = 32 amot (30 + 1 + 1). Race up this ramp was the original selection method.
learning.openQuestion: Why does the height matter? Because the priests ran up this ramp at full speed in the dark - a 32-amah ramp is significant enough that a push at the top could be fatal.

argumentFlow.label: Ramp height: 32 amot
argumentFlow.text: R. Yosi b. R. Yehuda: the altar ramp was 32 amot long: 30 amot of rise, plus 1 amah for the lip of the altar, plus 1 amah for the altar body.

misconceptions: The calculation 30+1+1=32 reflects the specific architectural features of the ramp: the 30-amah practical rise, the 1-amah lip (sovev), and 1-amah body measurement.

visualizableElements.description: The altar ramp (kevesh) with measurements labeled: 30 amot rise, 1 amah lip, 1 amah body = 32 total

quizSeeds.q: What was the total height of the altar ramp according to R. Yosi b. R. Yehuda, and how is it calculated?
quizSeeds.a: 32 amot: 30 amot of actual rise + 1 amah for the altar lip + 1 amah for the altar body.

sugyot.finalRuling: The altar ramp was 32 amot according to R. Yosi b. R. Yehuda.

glossary.en: The ramp leading up to the altar; priests ran up this ramp in the original competition for Temple services.

display.whats: The four lotteries of the Temple morning service: (1) who removes ashes; (2) who slaughters, sprinkles blood, removes ash, cleans the menorah and altar, and brings the limbs, flour, wine, and incense to the altar entrance; (3) the new priest designated for the incense; (4) who brings the limbs from the ramp to the altar. The lottery was held in the Chamber of Hewn Stone. The Gemara discusses how the lottery was conducted - counting priests who extended fingers.
display.hint: The four lotteries cover the complete morning service schedule. Lottery 3 specifically favors those who had not previously performed the incense service - because the incense made priests wealthy (the smoke's scent clung to them, giving them dignity), so the opportunity was rotated.

learning.summary: The Gemara describes the four Temple morning lotteries. The first covers ash removal; the second covers the main morning service sequence (13 participants); the third determines who offers the incense (with priority for those who had never done it before, since the incense brought blessing and wealth); the fourth covers bringing limbs to the altar. All lotteries were conducted in the Chamber of Hewn Stone.
learning.ahaMoment: The incense lottery (third) had a unique rule: if you had done it before, you were not eligible again. This was because the incense made priests wealthy - its scent clung to their clothes, giving them status. The Sages wanted equal distribution of this benefit.
learning.memoryAnchor: Four lotteries: (1) ash; (2) main service 13-person list; (3) incense for first-timers; (4) limbs. Chamber of Hewn Stone.
learning.openQuestion: The incense made priests wealthy and fragrant. The Gemara says 'a man who burned incense never became poor.' Why was this the one service with a first-timer preference?

argumentFlow.label: The four lotteries described
argumentFlow.text: Mishna: there were four lotteries daily. The first was for ash removal; the second for the main service (13 positions); the third for the incense (first-timers only); the fourth for carrying limbs to the altar.

argumentFlow.label: The second lottery's 13 positions
argumentFlow.text: The second lottery assigned 13 positions for the main morning service: slaughter, blood, two for the head, two for the tail, etc., the flour offering, and the wine libation.

argumentFlow.label: Third lottery: incense for first-timers
argumentFlow.text: The third lottery was exclusively for priests who had never performed the incense service before. The incense brought blessing and dignity to those who burned it.

argumentFlow.label: Why the incense lottery favored first-timers
argumentFlow.text: The incense was said never to have left its performer poor. The scent clung to the performer's clothing. Therefore the Sages spread this privilege broadly.

argumentFlow.label: Fourth lottery: carrying limbs
argumentFlow.text: The fourth lottery determined which priests carried the animal limbs from the altar ramp up to the altar proper. Any priest who had not yet participated that morning could join.

argumentFlow.label: Lottery procedure: counting extended fingers
argumentFlow.text: The lottery was conducted by counting priests who extended one or two fingers. The chief officer named a large number; counting began from a designated starting priest; whoever's count reached the named number performed the service.

misconceptions: The fourth lottery was not a replacement for the third - each covered distinct services. A priest could potentially participate in multiple lotteries in one morning.
The 'no repeat for incense' rule applied to the third lottery specifically - it ensured broad distribution of the blessing associated with the incense service.

visualizableElements.description: Four lotteries in order: (1) ash removal, (2) 13-position main service, (3) incense for first-timers, (4) limbs to altar
The Chamber of Hewn Stone (lishkat hagazit) where the morning lottery was conducted
Priests extending fingers; an officer counting to a named number to determine who performs each service

quizSeeds.q: How many lotteries were held each morning in the Temple, and what did the third lottery cover?
quizSeeds.a: Four lotteries. The third lottery was for the incense (ketoret) service, limited to priests who had never performed it before, because the incense brought blessing and prosperity and the Sages wanted to spread this privilege broadly.

quizSeeds.q: How was the lottery physically conducted?
quizSeeds.a: The chief officer named a large number; priests extended fingers (one or two); counting began from a designated starting priest; whoever's count reached the named number won that service.

sugyot.finalRuling: Four lotteries were held: ash removal, 13-position morning service, incense (first-timers only), and limbs carrying.

glossary.en: Chamber of Hewn Stone; the seat of the Sanhedrin and the location of the morning lottery.
Incense; its daily burning was believed to bring wealth to the priest who performed it. The third lottery was reserved for first-timers.

display.whats: The Saul-David biblical narrative digression continues (from 22a). The Gemara examines why Saul merited the kingship (he was humble), why he lost it (he disobeyed Samuel over Amalek), and the contrast with David who kept it despite serious sin. Key: Saul said 'I have sinned' but it was not genuine repentance. The Gemara also discusses whether a Torah scholar should avenge like a snake - i.e., quietly without forgetting.
display.hint: The Saul digression is not random. The counting of Israel (which Saul did, like the Temple count-off) is the textual hook, but the real interest is the question of when a king/person loses divine favor permanently. The answer the Gemara develops is about the quality of repentance and the nature of the sin's public impact.

learning.summary: Why did Saul deserve the kingdom? He was humble: 'Am I not from the smallest tribe?' Why did he lose it? He disobeyed the Amalek command and failed to repent genuinely. David sinned gravely but repented immediately and genuinely - 'I have sinned against God.' The Gemara explores the difference in the quality of their repentance and the public vs. private nature of their sins.
learning.ahaMoment: The key insight: both Saul and David said 'I have sinned.' But Saul said it to Samuel (wanting Samuel not to shame him publicly), while David said it to Nathan and then immediately to God. The audience of repentance matters: public sin requires public, not private, confession.
learning.memoryAnchor: Saul: 'I have sinned' - to Samuel, wanting cover. David: 'I have sinned to God' - genuine, immediate. Same words, opposite outcomes.
learning.openQuestion: Is the Gemara teaching that all repentance requires publicness? Or specifically that political leaders (kings) must repent publicly because their sins are public?

argumentFlow.label: Why did Saul merit the kingship?
argumentFlow.text: Saul was chosen because he was humble: 'am I not from Benjamin, the smallest tribe, and from the least of families?' His humility made him worthy.

argumentFlow.label: Why did Saul lose the kingship permanently?
argumentFlow.text: Despite saying 'I have sinned,' Saul's sin was not forgiven. The Gemara probes: was his repentance genuine? What made his sin unforgivable?

argumentFlow.label: Saul's repentance vs. David's repentance
argumentFlow.text: Saul said 'I have sinned' to Samuel - but then added 'honor me now before the elders.' This shows his concern was his reputation, not genuine contrition. David said 'I have sinned to God' (to Nathan) and accepted the consequences without protecting his image.

argumentFlow.label: The nature of Saul's sin: public violation
argumentFlow.text: Saul publicly disobeyed a public divine command (destroy Amalek) in a way that all Israel could see. This required a public divine repudiation. Private repentance cannot undo public divine commands.

argumentFlow.label: Additional details on Saul's sin
argumentFlow.text: Further analysis of Saul sparing Agag and the best animals despite explicit command to destroy everything.

argumentFlow.label: Why David kept the kingdom despite sin
argumentFlow.text: David's sin (adultery, murder by proxy) was private. His repentance was genuine and immediate. The kingdom, which represented the divine covenant, was not revoked because the repentance was complete.

argumentFlow.label: A Torah scholar must avenge like a snake
argumentFlow.text: A talmid chacham who is insulted should not ignore it - he should avenge like a snake (quietly, without forgetting). This principle is brought in connection with a discussion of Saul's overly passive response.

argumentFlow.label: The snake's method of avenging
argumentFlow.text: A snake does not strike immediately but remembers and eventually avenges. Similarly, a Torah scholar who is wronged should not immediately retaliate but should not forget either.

argumentFlow.label: Closing the Saul-David comparison
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara closes the comparison: Saul's failure was theological (disobeying a public divine mandate without genuine repentance); David's sin was human (passion, poor judgment) with genuine immediate repentance.

narrative: Saul's humility at selection; his failure at Amalek; David's genuine repentance

misconceptions: 'Avenge like a snake' is not a general endorsement of vengeance. It is specifically about a talmid chacham who has been publicly shamed - he must not simply absorb the shame, as that would undermine Torah's dignity.
Saul said 'I have sinned' three times in Samuel. The Gemara does not deny this but argues that the follow-up requests (honor me, come with me) show the repentance was incomplete.

visualizableElements.description: Saul: 'I have sinned - now honor me before the elders.' David: 'I have sinned to God' - then accepted Nathan's rebuke without protest.
Saul standing before Samuel, asking for honor despite his admission of sin

quizSeeds.q: What was different about Saul's repentance compared to David's, according to the Gemara?
quizSeeds.a: Saul said 'I have sinned' but immediately added 'now honor me before the elders' - showing concern for his image rather than genuine contrition. David said 'I have sinned to God' to Nathan and accepted the consequences without self-protection.

quizSeeds.q: What does it mean that a Torah scholar must 'avenge like a snake'?
quizSeeds.a: A talmid chacham who is publicly insulted should not passively ignore it - he should quietly remember the wrong and eventually address it, as a snake strikes eventually without immediate outburst.

sugyot.finalRuling: Saul's repentance was incomplete (protecting his image); David's was genuine. A talmid chacham must not passively absorb public shame.

glossary.en: Torah scholar; one who must maintain the dignity of Torah against those who demean it.

display.whats: Continuing the Saul and David analysis: a dispute about Saul's additional sin (the slaughter of the priests of Nov), which David later claimed was his fault (he triggered it by fleeing to Nov). David says: 'I am guilty of the deaths of your father's house.' The Gemara also discusses Absalom's revolt and asks why David did not defend himself militarily against his own son.
display.hint: David's admission of guilt over Nov is remarkable - he takes personal responsibility for a disaster he did not commit. This is the opposite of Saul's self-protective repentance. The Absalom question further reveals David's character: he would not use military force against his own son, even when Absalom was in rebellion.

learning.summary: The Gemara continues: why did Saul massacre the priests of Nov? Because Doeg informed him that they had helped David. But David says: 'I am responsible for the deaths of your father's house (the Nob priests)' - he accepts moral responsibility even when he was not the direct cause. Why did David not fight Absalom militarily? He could not bear the thought of killing his own son.
learning.ahaMoment: David's accepting responsibility for the Nov massacre is a different kind of greatness from military victory. He did not cause it directly, yet he owns the moral chain of causation. This is the opposite of Saul, who minimized his responsibility for Amalek.
learning.memoryAnchor: David says 'I caused Nov' even though Saul gave the order. Accepts indirect moral causation. Refuses to fight Absalom.
learning.openQuestion: Is David's refusal to fight Absalom admirable (parental love) or a failure of leadership (allowing rebellion to spread)? The Gemara explores both.

argumentFlow.label: The Nov massacre and David's guilt
argumentFlow.text: Saul massacred the priests of Nov after Doeg told him David had received help there. Later, David tells Evyatar: 'I knew Doeg would inform; I caused the deaths of your father's house.'

argumentFlow.label: Why Saul's sin of Nov was so severe
argumentFlow.text: The priests of Nov were killed on Saul's order based on Doeg's false testimony. This was not just a political act - it was mass killing of kohanim. The Gemara adds it to Saul's list of unforgivable acts.

argumentFlow.label: David and Absalom: why no military response?
argumentFlow.text: When Absalom revolted, David did not immediately mobilize the military against him. The Gemara asks why - David was fully capable of fighting. Answer: he could not bring himself to order military force against his own son.

argumentFlow.label: Further analysis of David and Absalom
argumentFlow.text: David's response to Absalom's rebellion reveals the limits of his authority - even a great king cannot fully suppress his parental instincts. The Gemara explores whether this was weakness or virtue.

argumentFlow.label: Closing the Saul-David digression
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara wraps up the biblical narrative comparing Saul and David, preparing to return to the main topic of Temple lottery procedures.

misconceptions: David did not literally kill the priests of Nov - Saul did. But David accepts the chain of causation (he fled to Nov, triggering the information that led to the massacre) as making him morally complicit.
David's refusal to fight Absalom militarily eventually had to give way - Joab's forces did kill Absalom. But David's tears ('My son Absalom!') show this was not what he wanted.

visualizableElements.description: David telling Evyatar the survivor of Nov: 'I knew Doeg was there; I am responsible for your father's house'
David weeping over Absalom: unable to issue military orders against his own son despite the political necessity

quizSeeds.q: What does David say about the massacre of the priests of Nov, and what does this reveal about his character?
quizSeeds.a: David tells Evyatar (the sole survivor): 'I knew Doeg would inform; I am responsible for the deaths of your father's house.' He accepts indirect moral causation even though Saul gave the actual order - the opposite of Saul's self-protective behavior.

quizSeeds.q: Why did David not immediately fight Absalom militarily when Absalom revolted?
quizSeeds.a: David could not bring himself to order military force against his own son. His parental love paralyzed his political response.

sugyot.finalRuling: David accepted moral responsibility for indirect harm (Nov) and refused to fight his son Absalom directly. The Saul-David comparison concludes.

glossary.en: The city of priests where David received help when fleeing Saul; later massacred on Saul's orders based on Doeg's report.
Absalom; David's son who led a revolt against him. David could not bring himself to fight his own son.
Altar ramp; 32 amot long; site of the original priestly race.
Chamber of Hewn Stone; location of the morning lottery.
Incense; its burning brought blessing. Third lottery reserved for first-timers.
Torah scholar; must avenge like a snake when publicly shamed.
City of priests massacred on Saul's order; David accepted indirect responsibility.
David's son who revolted; David could not fight him militarily.
